speaker: ALEX
text: This is Git Going with GitHub, episode 26: GitHub Projects Deep Dive. I am Alex. By the end of this episode, GitHub Projects Deep Dive should feel less like a wall of GitHub words and more like a set of moves you can trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I am here for the practical questions: what should I listen for, what can go wrong, and what is the next calm move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Today we are working on this: Project boards, table and roadmap views, custom fields, cross-repo management. I want the learner to leave with a mental map, not just a remembered path through buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal is understanding first, then action, then confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. We are building a rhythm: orient yourself, take one intentional action, then verify what changed before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Boards, Tables, Roadmaps, Automations, and Accessible Navigation: GitHub Projects is GitHub's built-in project management system. The next useful detail is concrete: It connects issues and pull requests from one or more repositories into a living, filterable view that your whole team can see and act on.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Here is the learner-facing version. GitHub has two generations of project tooling. Put another way, the current generation - called Projects (v2) - is what you'll find on any repository or organization page today. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 2. Creating a Project: projects can be created at the organization level (shared across all repos in an org) or at the repository level (scoped to one repo).

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Create from an Organization has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Navigate to your organization page (github.com/your-org); Select the Projects tab; Activate "New project" button; and Choose a template (blank table, blank board, feature release, etc.) or start empty. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is give the project a name and select "Create project". That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Create from a Repository has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to any repository. Step two is select the Projects tab. Step three is activate "Link a project" or "New project". Step four is follow the same template/name flow. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Learning Cards: Creating a Project has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. The "New project" button is on the Projects tab of any org or repo page -- Tab to it and press Enter to open the template modal. Template cards in the modal are navigable with arrow keys -- each template announces its name and description; press Enter on your choice. After creation, you land on an empty project -- Tab to the "+ Add item" button at the bottom to start adding issues. The green "New project" button on the Projects tab is high-contrast and easy to spot even at high zoom. Template cards in the creation modal are displayed as a grid -- zoom in to read the titles and descriptions before selecting one. After the project is created, the empty Table view has a "+ Add item" row at the bottom -- it can scroll off screen at high zoom, so scroll down to find it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Table View is still useful because a spreadsheet-style grid where each row is an issue, PR, or draft item. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Columns are fields (Title, Status, Assignee, Labels, Priority, custom fields you create).

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put Screen reader output per row into plain language. "Fix keyboard trap in modal dialog Status: In Progress Assignee: alice Priority: High Labels: bug, accessibility".

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Each column represents a status value. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Cards can be dragged between columns (or updated via keyboard by editing the Status field).

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Change a card's column without dragging has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is enter to open the card. Step two is navigate to the Status field in the right panel. Step three is activate the Status dropdown. Step four is select the new column value. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, escape to close. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Roadmap View: Items appear as bars spanning their start/due dates. The next useful detail is concrete: Iterations and milestones can be visualized against real calendar time.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Setting dates has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Add a Date custom field (e.g., "Start Date", "Due Date") or use a Milestone field. Items without dates appear in the ungrouped section.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: The Three Layouts has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Table view is the most accessible layout -- navigate with arrow keys (up/down for rows, left/right for columns) and press Enter to open an item's detail panel. Board view drag-and-drop is not keyboard accessible -- change a card's column by opening it (Enter), navigating to the Status field, and selecting a new value from the dropdown. Roadmap view is chart-based and not directly readable -- use Table view for all data access and editing, then switch to Roadmap only for visual context if needed. Table view works best at high zoom because it is a standard grid -- Board view columns can overflow horizontally and require scrolling. Board view cards show title, labels, and assignee -- zoom in on individual cards to read the small label badges and avatar icons. Roadmap view uses horizontal bars on a calendar timeline -- the bars can be narrow at default zoom; increase zoom or switch to Table for the same date data in text form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on 4. Custom Fields. This is one of Projects v2's most powerful features. This is the part to say slowly: You can add fields beyond the GitHub defaults (Title, Assignee, Labels, Milestone, Repository). The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Field Types. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: text: Free-form notes for things like acceptance criteria or design links. The listener should be able to check this: Number: Numeric values such as story points or estimates (for example, "Points: 3").

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating a Custom Field. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating a Custom Field has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, in Table view, scroll right to the + button at the end of the column header row. Then, activate the + button. After that, select a field type. Finally, name the field and configure options (for select fields, type each option, press Enter to add more). Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Creating a Custom Field, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Confirm - the field appears as a new column. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Editing a Field Value on an Item. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Editing a Field Value on an Item has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: In Table view, navigate to the cell where the field intersects the row. Then: Enter or Space to activate the editor. Next: Type value (text/number) or select from dropdown (single select). Last: Enter or Tab to confirm. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Add an Existing Issue or PR. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Add an Existing Issue or PR into plain language. The issue itself will now show which projects it belongs to in its sidebar. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Activate the "+ Add item" button at the bottom of a table/column; Type to trigger the issue/PR search; Type a keyword or issue number; and Select the item - it's added to the project. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Add an Existing Issue or PR, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 1 checks: The issue/PR is now tracked in the project; it still lives in its repository. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid issue habit is to read the title, the body, and the timeline before acting. You are listening for the requested action, the missing evidence, and the person who needs a response.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Add a Draft Issue. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Draft issues live only inside the project (not in any repository) until you promote them. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: When to use drafts: Capturing ideas during planning before you're ready to commit them to a repo.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Activate "+ Add item"; Type the title directly (no ); Press Enter - a draft row appears; and Open the draft → "Convert to issue" button → select the repository. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Promote a Draft to an Issue. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Promote a Draft to an Issue has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is open the draft item (Enter on the row). Step two is activate "Convert to issue" in the panel. Step three is select the target repository from the dropdown. Step four is confirm - the draft becomes a real issue in that repo. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Bulk Edit Items. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Bulk Edit Items has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, check the checkbox on each row (first column, Space to toggle). Then, a bulk action bar appears at the top. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. Projects v2 has native automation rules - no GitHub Actions YAML required. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Accessing Automations. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Accessing Automations has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Select the. menu (project overflow) in the top-right; Select "Workflows"; and You'll see a list of built-in automation rules you can enable/disable. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on Available Built-In Workflows. Item added to project: When an item is manually added, its Status is set to a value you choose. This is the part to say slowly: Item reopened: When an issue or pull request is reopened, its Status changes back (for example, to "In Progress").

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Setting Up Auto-Add. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: this is the most powerful workflow for maintainers. The listener should be able to check this: Now any matching issue in linked repos is added to your project the moment it's filed - no manual triage step required.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is in Workflows, select "Auto-add to project". Step two is toggle it On. Step three is configure the filter: e.g., label:accessibility is:issue adds any new issue with the accessibility label automatically. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat GitHub Actions Integration as decoration. For more complex automations, Projects v2 has a full GraphQL API and works with GitHub Actions. That is not trivia. See Appendix Q: GitHub Actions and Workflows for the foundation, and the GitHub documentation on automating projects for advanced patterns. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Auto-assign items based on repository when added. Set due dates based on linked milestone. Send Slack notifications when high-priority items are added.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 7. Iterations (Sprints). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 7. Iterations (Sprints) is still useful because an iteration field lets you group work into time-boxed cycles - sprints, weeks, or any cadence you choose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating an Iteration Field. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating an Iteration Field has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Add a new field (the + column button in Table view); Select "Iteration" as the field type; Name it (e.g., "Sprint"); and Set the iteration duration (1 week, 2 weeks, custom). If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Creating an Iteration Field, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 1 checks: GitHub automatically generates current and upcoming iterations. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Using Iterations. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Using Iterations has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Each item can be assigned to an iteration via the Iteration field. In Board view, you can group by iteration to see a sprint board. In Roadmap view, iterations appear as blocks on the timeline. Burndown: No built-in burndown chart, but the Insights charts (see below) can show progress.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Iteration Insights. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Iteration Insights has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is select the Insights tab (the bar chart icon). Step two is select "Current iteration". Step three is see: items by status, completion rate, items added vs closed over time. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 8. Views and Filters. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 8. Views and Filters: A view is a saved configuration of layout + filters + grouping + sort. The next useful detail is concrete: You can have many views on one project, each showing a different slice of the data.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Creating a View. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Creating a View has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Activate the "+ New view" button in the view tab bar. Then: Choose layout (Table, Board, Roadmap). Next: Configure filters, grouping, sort. Last: The view saves automatically with a default name - rename by clicking/activating the tab label. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Filter Syntax. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Filter Syntax: the filter bar accepts a query language similar to GitHub's issue search. That matters in practice: status:In Progress shows only items with that Status value.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Grouping. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Grouping. In Table or Board view, you can group rows. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Select "Group by" in the view configuration menu; Choose any field: Status, Assignee, Priority, Iteration, Label, Repository; and Items are collapsible into group sections. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Sorting. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: multiple sort levels are supported.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Priority ascending, then due date ascending.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. Do not treat 9. Cross-Repository Projects as decoration. A single project can track issues and PRs from multiple repositories - including repos in different organizations (if you have access).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Add a Repository to a Project. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Add a Repository to a Project has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: In project settings (the. overflow menu → "Settings" ). Then: Select "Manage access" → "Add repository". Next: Search and select additional repositories. Last: Items from those repos can now be added to the project. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Put Using the Repository Field into plain language. Common use case: An organization project tracking all accessibility bugs across every repo, labeled accessibility, with Auto-add enabled. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: Project Home Page (list of projects) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like 3 → Jump to project titles (they are h3 links); Enter → Open a project. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because NVDA note: Use Browse mode (NVDA+Space to toggle) to read all cell content, then App mode (NVDA+Space again) for interactive editing. That gives the learner a foothold: NVDA announces cell content as you navigate with arrow keys.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 11. Accessibility Agents: /project-status. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 11. Accessibility Agents: /project-status: The /project-status command from Accessibility Agents gives you a live project board overview directly in VS Code Copilot Chat, without opening a browser.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. What It Shows has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Items per column (Todo, In Progress, Done, etc.) with assignees and average age. Blocked items (labeled "blocked" or flagged with a field value). Stale items - in the same column for 7+ days with no activity. Unassigned items in active columns. Team member workload (anyone with 5+ items in progress). Sprint/iteration progress if an Iteration field is configured.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Example Output. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Example Output: GitHub Project: Accessibility Agents Roadmap (3 active views). That matters in practice: After the overview: "Want to see a specific column or check a team member's workload?".

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Stale: "Update screen reader navigation guide" - In Review for 9 days, no activity. Unassigned: "Triage accessibility bug backlog" in In Progress.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. When to Use It has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. During standup: quick status snapshot without leaving VS Code. Before filing a new issue: see if it's already tracked. During sprint planning: identify stale and blocked items before the next cycle. After a weekend: catch up on what moved while you were away.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Exercise 1: Create a Personal Tracking Project. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exercise 1: Create a Personal Tracking Project has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is create a new project at the organization level: name it "My Workshop Contributions". Step two is add the Board layout. Step three is add a custom Priority field with options: P0, P1, P2. Step four is add your fork of accessibility-agents as a linked repository. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Exercise 1: Create a Personal Tracking Project, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, add any open issue you've filed to the project. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Exercise 2: Set Up Automation. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exercise 2: Set Up Automation has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, in your project's Workflows, enable "Item closed" → Status: Done. Then, file a test issue in your fork. After that, close that issue. Finally, verify it moved to "Done" automatically in the project board. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Exercise 3: Create a Sprint View. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exercise 3: Create a Sprint View has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Add an Iteration field named "Sprint" with 2-week iterations. Then: Assign your existing project items to the current sprint. Next: Switch to Roadmap view - observe the items on the timeline. Last: Check Insights to see the current iteration burndown. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Exercise 4: Cross-Repo Project (Advanced). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put Exercise 4: Cross-Repo Project (Advanced) into plain language. From Chapter 9: For the introductory coverage of Labels, Milestones, and Projects including screen reader basics for getting started, see Chapter 9: Labels, Milestones, and Projects. The useful version is: Next: Appendix S: Releases and Insights Back: Appendix Q: GitHub Actions Teaching chapter: Chapter 09: Labels, Milestones, and Projects.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Create a cross-repo project connected to two of your repositories; Add the Repository field to the Table view; Create a view filtered to is:issue label:accessibility; and Set up Auto-add: any issue with the accessibility label in those repos goes to this project automatically. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 26. Next in the series is episode 27, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
